skill.name: mastra
skill.description: Guide for building Mastra applications with current documentation, embedded package docs, setup workflows, migrations, agents, tools, memory, RAG, and troubleshooting patterns.
skill.summary: Build Mastra agents and workflows using verified current API documentation.
skill.author: mastra-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and environment-variable patterns, but review shows they are documentation examples and setup instructions rather than hidden executable code. No prompt-injection attempt, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious persistence behavior was found. Publish with a warning because the skill recommends package-manager commands, remote documentation fetching, and API-key environment setup.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users or agents to run package-manager commands with @latest, including project creation, dependency installation, and codemods. This is legitimate for Mastra setup, but it executes code from external package registries and should require user approval in restricted environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible setup and migration instructions, not hidden execution paths. Confidence is high that the behavior is intentional and legitimate, but @latest package execution creates real supply-chain exposure.

medium_findings.title: API Key Setup Guidance Needs Caution
medium_findings.description: The setup guide asks for provider API keys and shows .env-based configuration. This is normal for AI application setup, but marketplace users should avoid pasting secrets into chat and should prefer local environment managers or secret stores.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text clearly references API-key handling, including a line asking the user to provide a key for automatic setup. I found no exfiltration endpoint or hidden collection logic, so the concern is handling practice rather than confirmed theft.

low_findings.title: Remote Documentation Fetching Is Explicit and Domain-Scoped
low_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to fetch documentation from mastra.ai and GitHub links. These hardcoded URLs are expected for a framework documentation skill and do not show suspicious third-party collection endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are visible official documentation or project resources. I did not find evidence of credential transfer, opaque URLs, or unrelated remote services.

low_findings.title: Static Code-Execution Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external-command and weak-crypto static matches are caused by markdown fences, inline commands, file extensions, model names, or troubleshooting examples. They are not runnable scripts shipped with the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed examples are in markdown reference files and are presented as user-visible instructions. No executable source file, install hook, or hidden command launcher was found in the scanned file set.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell commands for package installation, project initialization, Docker, TypeScript checks, and development servers. These commands are transparent and relevant, but agents should request approval before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present and repeated across setup documentation. The risk is operational execution of visible commands, not covert command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents API keys and database URLs in .env examples. Placeholders are used for provider keys, while the Postgres example contains a local development password.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are normal development configuration snippets and not active secret access. They still merit disclosure because users may copy secrets into chat or commit .env files accidentally.

content.user_title: Build Mastra Apps With Current APIs
content.value_statement: Mastra APIs change quickly, and outdated examples can break agent projects. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to verify docs before building agents, tools, workflows, memory, and RAG features.
actual_capabilities: Finds current Mastra APIs through embedded package docs when dependencies are installed.
Uses remote Mastra documentation when local packages are unavailable.
Guides setup for new Mastra projects, TypeScript configuration, agents, tools, and workflows.
Explains common Mastra errors and how to verify imports, models, storage, memory, and tool schemas.
Supports migration planning by checking official migration docs and installed package definitions.
limitations: It does not execute Mastra code by itself; commands require the agent or user to run them.
Remote documentation may differ from the installed package version.
It depends on local node_modules content when embedded docs are used.
It does not manage secrets or validate production security settings.

use_cases.title: Create a Mastra Starter Project
use_cases.description: Set up a TypeScript Mastra project with correct package scripts, model format, and a simple agent structure.

use_cases.title: Debug Broken Mastra Code
use_cases.description: Resolve type errors, missing package exports, tool validation issues, memory setup problems, and model configuration mistakes.

use_cases.title: Plan a Version Migration
use_cases.description: Compare current package APIs with official migration guidance before updating Mastra dependencies and codemods.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Mastra project plan for a TypeScript agent app. Check the current docs first and explain each command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Agent With a Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Mastra skill to build an agent with one validated tool. Verify the current Agent and createTool signatures before writing files.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Type Error
prompt_templates.prompt: My Mastra project has a TypeScript error. Use embedded docs if available, identify the outdated API, and propose the smallest fix.

prompt_templates.title: Upgrade a Mastra Codebase
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Mastra version upgrade. Check official migration docs, inspect installed APIs, list risky changes, and define validation steps.

output_examples.input: Create a Mastra weather agent project.
output_examples.output: A setup plan with dependency checks, TypeScript settings, environment key guidance, agent and tool file locations, and a Studio test step.

output_examples.input: The Agent constructor rejects my tools property.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis that compares the installed type definition with the current code and recommends the exact configuration change to test.

output_examples.input: Upgrade my Mastra project to the latest version.
output_examples.output: A migration checklist covering backups, official docs, dependency updates, codemod review, API verification, and test commands.

best_practices: Verify installed API signatures before writing or changing Mastra code.
Ask before running package-manager, Docker, migration, or development-server commands.
Store API keys locally and avoid pasting real secrets into chat prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on memory for Mastra APIs when docs or type definitions are available.
Do not run @latest installers or codemods without user approval and review.
Do not commit .env files or hardcode provider API keys in source files.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps build Mastra agents, tools, workflows, memory features, RAG features, and project setup flows using current docs.

faq.question: Why does it check embedded docs first?
faq.answer: Embedded docs match the installed package version, so they are more reliable than memory or generic examples.

faq.question: Can it use remote documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It points agents to mastra.ai documentation when local packages are missing or conceptual guidance is needed.

faq.question: Does it need my API keys?
faq.answer: Mastra apps need provider keys, but you should set them locally and avoid sharing real secrets in chat.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. The commands and network access are visible documentation steps, not hidden code.

faq.question: What should I review before running commands?
faq.answer: Review package installs, codemods, Docker commands, and any command that changes files or starts services.
